title: Basketry materials
subtitle: Choosing, preparing, and sourcing willow, rush, and hedgerow material
excerpt: The material is the basket. A basket woven from the wrong willow or improperly prepared rush will be stiff, brittle, or short-lived regardless of the quality of the weaving. This guide covers the three main forms of commercial basket willow, harvested rush, hedgerow alternatives, and the soaking and preparation that makes each material workable.
sourceNotes: Encyclopaedia Britannica 11th edition (1911) for willow varieties, buff preparation, and commercial basket willow cultivation. Cassell's Household Guide (1869) for rush harvesting and preparation. Wright, Profitable Fruit Growing (1882) for willow cultivation and Somerset basket-making.

content.text: Commercial basket willow in Britain comes in three forms, each with a distinct appearance and different preparation requirements.

is the most common: boiled to strip the bark, then dried, it has a consistent tan-yellow surface and bends evenly after soaking. It is the best starting material for beginners because its behaviour is predictable.
is dried with its bark on; the surface is rougher and darker and the rods are slightly stiffer than buff willow at the same diameter. It is traditional for outdoor baskets and garden work.

is stripped fresh without boiling and has the finest, smoothest surface of the three; it is the traditional material for shopping baskets intended for display. Stripping fresh willow in quantity is labour-intensive and is only done commercially in spring.

All dried willow must be soaked before use. The standard guidance is 1 minute of soaking per centimetre of rod length: a 75 cm rod needs about 75 minutes in cold water, or 45 minutes in warm water. After soaking, wrap the rods in a damp cloth and leave for 30 minutes to 1 hour to allow the moisture to penetrate evenly; rods soaked without the mellow period will be flexible on the surface but brittle at the core and will crack under a tight bend. Check by bending a test rod to a right angle; if it bends without cracking, the batch is ready. Thick base sticks (6 mm and above) need double the soaking time of thin weaving rods at the same length.

Rush seating uses dried bulrush (Typha latifolia, common bulrush, or Scirpus lacustris, common club-rush). Both grow at the margins of slow rivers, ponds, and ditches across Britain. Harvesting season is July to August, when the rush has reached full height but before the seed heads open. Cut at water level, spread in a single layer on grass in the sun for 3 to 4 days, then bundle loosely and store in a dry ventilated place. Dried rush keeps indefinitely if kept dry; damp storage causes mould and the rush becomes brittle. For use, soak in cold water for 5 minutes, drain, and wrap in a damp cloth for 20 minutes. Over-soaking makes rush slimy and difficult to twist; 5 minutes is enough for most material.

Before willow could be bought by the bundle, basket makers used whatever flexible rods grew nearby. Hazel, osier willow cut from pollards, dogwood, and field maple all worked. Hazel makes a coarser basket than cultivated willow. The rods vary in width and the surface is rougher. Even so, hazel hurdles and garden panels use the same over-under weave as a proper basket. Hazel is free from any managed coppice. Bramble, with the thorns stripped off, was used in a pinch and a few rural craftspeople still use it. These hedgerow materials weave fresh. They do not need the prep that buff or brown willow needs.

Cultivated basket willow in Britain comes primarily from Somerset (the Somerset Levels), where Salix triandra and Salix viminalis are grown as two- to three-year coppice. Rods are sold by weight in bolts (bundles). A bolt of buff willow weighs approximately 2 kg. For a beginner project such as a small oval basket, one bolt of mixed lengths (60 to 90 cm) is sufficient. Longer rods (90 to 120 cm) are needed for larger baskets and for making handles. Specialist suppliers sell by variety and length; for a first order, a mixed beginner bundle is easier to work with than buying separate lots of stakes, base sticks, and weaving rods.
